_metadata.description: Formative vs. summative assessment definitions, Poorvu seven formative principles, Poorvu five summative recommendations, AI-resistant assessment design, and alignment-to-outcomes audit logic.
_metadata.owner: Chaz Clark (BYUI faculty-development; Architects of Learning week on assessments)
_metadata.runtime_strategy_rationale: Body MD ~12,400 chars / 4 ≈ 3,100 tokens — within the 'either' band per runtime_strategy_rules. Set to read_at_runtime because (a) the file is one of ~12 sibling knowledge files in canvas-toolbox that an agent loads selectively per report focus, not a stable cache-friendly prefix on every call, and (b) consumers (canvas_course_expert, ira_program_alignment) cite back to source materials and benefit from first-class retrieval citations (Anthropic Citations / OpenAI file_search annotations).

scope: Formative vs. summative assessment definitions, design principles, AI-resistant design, and alignment-to-outcomes audit logic. Boundaries: does not cover backward design (see backwards_design_knowledge) or CLO well-formedness (see outcomes_quality_knowledge).

facts.term: Formative Assessment
facts.definition: An assessment employed while learning is ongoing to collect information about whether course objectives are being advanced and how teaching can be improved. Aims to identify strengths, challenges, and misconceptions and to close those gaps. May involve students assessing themselves, peers, or even the instructor. Forms include writing, quizzes, conversation, polls, low-stakes group work, weekly quizzes, 1-minute reflections, homework, surveys.
citations: Yale Poorvu Center, *Formative & Summative Assessments*, p.2

facts.term: Summative Assessment
facts.definition: An assessment used by instructors to evaluate student learning, knowledge, proficiency, or success at the conclusion of an instructional period (unit, course, or program). Almost always formally graded, often heavily weighted. Common forms: exams, papers, presentations, final projects, final reports, final grades. Maki (2002) frames the goal as 'summing up' student learning through measurable outcomes.
citations: Yale Poorvu Center, *Formative & Summative Assessments*, p.2; Maki 2002

facts.term: Alignment Claim
facts.definition: Both assessment types must allow instructors AND students to monitor progress toward achieving course learning objectives. An assessment that does not let either party monitor progress toward a specific CLO is not an assessment — it is a graded artifact. Every assessment must be traceable to a named CLO.
citations: Yale Poorvu Center, *Formative & Summative Assessments*, p.2 (opening definition)

facts.term: Formative Principle 1 — Provide clear criteria for good performance
facts.definition: Explain criteria; encourage discussion through office hours, post-grade peer review, or exam/assignment wrappers; hold class-wide conversations on criteria at strategic moments throughout a term.

facts.term: Formative Principle 2 — Encourage self-reflection
facts.definition: Ask students to use course criteria to evaluate their own or a peer's work, and to share what kinds of feedback they find most valuable. Have them describe strengths and weaknesses through writing or group discussion.

facts.term: Formative Principle 3 — Give detailed, actionable feedback
facts.definition: Feedback should be specific, tied to predefined criteria, with opportunity to revise or apply feedback before final submission. Corrective and forward-looking, not merely evaluative. Examples: comments on multiple paper drafts, criterion discussions in 1-on-1 conferences, regular online quizzes.

facts.term: Formative Principle 4 — Encourage dialogue around the formative learning process
facts.definition: Mid-semester feedback and small-group feedback sessions where students reflect on the course and instructors respond. Students can identify examples of feedback comments they found useful and explain how they helped. Weave student feedback into the syllabus.

facts.term: Formative Principle 5 — Promote positive motivational beliefs and self-esteem
facts.definition: Students are more motivated when assured the instructor cares for their development. Allow rewrites/resubmissions to signal that an assignment is designed to promote development. Low-stakes assessments or anonymous automated online testing with unlimited resubmissions where appropriate.

facts.term: Formative Principle 6 — Provide opportunities to close the gap between current and desired performance
facts.definition: Clearly present opportunities to close gaps: resubmission opportunities, specific action points for writing or task-based assignments, sharing study or process strategies an instructor would use to succeed.

facts.term: Formative Principle 7 — Collect feedback to shape teaching
facts.definition: Instructors collect information to provide targeted feedback and instruction. Identify where students face challenges in assignments, exams, and written submissions. Promotes student metacognition. Poorvu staff can perform classroom observation or small-group feedback sessions. Incorporate feedback into future pedagogy.

facts.term: Summative Recommendation 1 — Share a Rubric
facts.definition: Use a rubric to lay out expected performance criteria for a range of grades. Rubrics describe what an ideal assignment looks like and summarize expected performance at the beginning of a term, providing clarity and expectations.

facts.term: Summative Recommendation 2 — Design Clear, Effective Questions
facts.definition: Essay questions should be clear and align with course materials and objectives while allowing students freedom to express knowledge creatively and in ways that honor how they digested, constructed, or mastered meaning.

facts.term: Summative Recommendation 3 — Assess Comprehensiveness
facts.definition: Effective summative assessments give students an opportunity to consider the totality of the course content — making broad connections, demonstrating synthesized skills, and exploring deeper concepts that drive or found a course's ideas. Select assessment type (exam, final paper, project) such that students can demonstrate learning and conceptual understanding.

facts.term: Summative Recommendation 4 — Make Parameters Clear
facts.definition: Length, depth of response, time and date, and grading standards must be well-defined. Knowledge assessed must relate clearly to content covered in the course. Provide required space and support for students with disabilities.

facts.term: Summative Recommendation 5 — Grade Blindly when bias risk is high
facts.definition: When goal is truly unbiased results for the summative assessment, consider blind grading techniques. When goal is feedback that speaks to a student's term-long trajectory, know whose work you grade.

facts.term: AI-Resistant Assessment Thesis
facts.definition: In the AI era, a polished submission is no longer evidence of learning. Assessment design must produce student-owned evidence — not merely an artifact that could have been student-produced. Achieved via productive friction: staged drafts, oral defenses, process documentation, revision cycles. Shift from artifact-as-evidence to process-as-evidence.
citations: Hardman, P. (2024), *Redesigning Instruction & Assessment in the Age of AI*, drphilippahardman.substack.com — saved HTML did not extract body; thesis from title/subtitle plus Kassorla *Inverted Bloom's* (see inverted_blooms_knowledge.md)

facts.term: BYUI Constraint — No Sunday Due Dates
facts.definition: BYUI courses must not place summative or formative deadlines on Sunday. Faith/values concern is non-negotiable. Design weekly assessment rhythms accordingly.
citations: Chaz Clark project context (BYUI faculty-development); not in Poorvu or Hardman sources

facts.term: BYUI Constraint — Human in the Loop (HITL) for AI-Mediated Assessment
facts.definition: Any AI use in feedback, grading, or formative scaffolding must include a human in the loop on consequential decisions. Non-negotiable.
citations: Chaz Clark project context (BYUI faculty-development); not in Poorvu or Hardman sources

facts.term: Audit Tag — assessment_type
facts.definition: Classifier emitted by canvas_course_expert and ira_program_alignment when this knowledge fires. Values: formative | summative | mixed | decorative. 'decorative' flags an assessment that measures nothing actionable (no CLO traceback, no signal to instructor or student).

facts.term: Audit Tag — assessment_alignment
facts.definition: Classifier indicating traceability of an assessment to a named CLO. Values: clo_traced | orphaned | under-specified.

sources: Yale Poorvu Center for Teaching and Learning. *Formative & Summative Assessments*. https://poorvucenter.yale.edu/teaching/teaching-resource-library/formative-summative-assessments (8 pages, retrieved 2026-05-12)

Hardman, P. (2024). *Redesigning Instruction & Assessment in the Age of AI*. https://drphilippahardman.substack.com/p/redesigning-instruction-and-assessment (saved HTML in pre_knowledge/assessments/; body content not extractable from saved JS-rendered snapshot — citation by title, subtitle, and stated thesis only)

Wiggins, G. *Understanding by Design* (10-min video transcript, ASCD; pre_knowledge/assessments/youtube_transcript.md)
Nicol, D. J., & Macfarlane-Dick, D. (2007). Formative assessment and self-regulated learning: A model and seven principles of good feedback practice (cited via Poorvu)
Ali, Q. I. (2024); Bakula (2010); King (2023); Maki (2002); McLaughlin & Yan (2017); Trumbull & Lash (2013) — secondary citations via Poorvu References & Resources, p.7-8

pre_run_checklist: Each entry in consumed_by[] resolves to an existing agent spec (canvas_course_expert.json verified; ira_program_alignment.md verified — no .json yet but agent exists)
post_run_checklist: /tmp/canvas_drafts/assessments_knowledge.md exists with title, scope, body (Terms), provenance, last_updated
/tmp/canvas_drafts/assessments_knowledge.json exists with shape=reference and non-empty facts[]

changes: Initial knowledge file generated by make_agent_knowledge v1.0 from source materials in canvas-toolbox/lib/agents/pre_knowledge/assessments/.

Ingests: Yale Poorvu Center *Formative & Summative Assessments* (8 pages, full); Hardman *Redesigning Instruction & Assessment in the Age of AI* (title/subtitle/thesis only — saved HTML body not extractable); Chaz notes + Wiggins UbD transcript (assessment-relevant portions).
Companion file: backwards_design_knowledge.md (UbD 3-stage framework, six facets, enduring understandings, essential questions).
